text: 52 Commission by a seafarer of any of the offenses enumerated below shall be a ground for 53 disciplinary action for which the corresponding penalties shall be imposed. 54 55 I. GRAVE OFFENSES. The following acts shall be considered as grave offenses which 56 shall merit the penalty of permanent disqualification from participation in the overseas 57 employment program: 58 59 a. Possession, sale, transport, distribution, delivery, supply, or use of prohibited 60 drugs, narcotics, or other drug-related contraband; 61 62 48 b. Sexual assault; 1 2 c. Rape; 3 4 d. Murder; 5 6 e. Acts of terrorism; or 7 8 f. Other heinous crimes. 9 10 II. SERIOUS OFFENSES. Serious offenses are punishable by One (1) Year and One (1) 11 Day to Two (2) Years suspension from participation in the overseas employment program: 12 13 a. Submitting, furnishing, or using false material information or documents or any 14 form of gross misrepresentation for the purpose of job application or employment; 15 16 b. Willfully making a false statement, report, certification or document for personal 17 gain or with intent to mislead or defraud the company or the authorities; 18 19 c. Commission of other acts amounting to a felony or crime involving moral turpitude 20 punishable by the laws of the Philippines, the flag state or the port state committed 21 during employment, including but not limited to: 22 23 i. Gun-running or illegal/unauthorized possession of firearms, ammunition, 24 explosives and/or related items thereto; or 25 26 ii. Possession of child pornographic materials. 27 28 d. Unjustified breach of employment contract by the seafarer; 29 30 e. Gross violation of LMA policies and regulations; 31 32 f. Deserting or attempting to desert; 33 34 g. Physically assaulting a superior officer, crew, passenger or other persons with 35 business onboard the ship; 36 37 h. Grave abuse of authority causing serious harm, injury, illness or death to any 38 person on board; 39 40 i. Inciting mutiny or malicious destruction of ship’s property or any activity which will 41 endanger the safety of the ship and its crew; 42 43 j. Drunkenness, such as being drunk or intoxicated while on duty, creating trouble 44 on board due to alcohol intoxication, or failure to perform assigned jobs due to 45 alcohol intoxication; 46 47 k. Connivance with or coddling of a stowaway; or 48 49 l. Conviction for violation of the Anti-Violence Against Women and Children Act (R.A. 50 No. 9262) or any other relevant laws involving gender-based violence. 51 52 III. LESS SERIOUS OFFENSES. Less serious offenses are punishable by Six (6) Months 53 to One (1) Year suspension from participation in the overseas employment program: 54 55 a. Unjustified refusal to join ship after all employment and travel documents have 56 been duly approved by the appropriate government agencies; 57 58 b. Violation of the Code of Discipline for Seafarers; 59 60 c. Other forms of misrepresentation relative to the overseas employment of the 61 seafarer; 62 49 d. Smuggling or violation of any customs rules and regulations of the Philippines and 1 of foreign ports, such as: 2 3 1. Smuggling any taxable item; 4 2. Abetting or conniving with others to commit smuggling; 5 3. Misdeclaration of or failing to declare articles leading to their seizure and fine 6 to the ship; 7 4. Misdeclaration of or failing to declare articles leading to their seizure but the 8 ship is not implicated; 9 5. Possession of pornographic materials other than child pornography leading 10 to their confiscation and fine to the ship; and 11 6. Any other violation of customs rules and regulations which implicates the 12 ship; 13 14 e. Advising, assisting or persuading another to desert; 15 16 f. Abandoning or leaving one’s post or duty without being properly relieved; 17 18 g. Leaving the ship without permission from responsible officers during or outside 19 working hours; 20 21 h. Entrusting to others assigned duties without authority of a superior officer; 22 23 i. Sleeping on post while on duty; 24 25 j. Insubordination or any act of disobedience to lawful orders of a superior officer; 26 27 k. Attempting to assault a superior officer, crew, passenger or other persons with 28 business at the ship; 29 30 l. Behaving with disrespect towards a superior officer; 31 32 m. Insulting a superior officer by words or deeds; 33 34 n. Inciting another to commit insubordination; 35 36 o. Creating trouble outside the ship’s premises that unjustly implicates the ship; 37 38 p. Any form of gambling, including online gambling, that results in fighting or any 39 incident as to upset the harmonious relationship on board the ship or any other 40 form of gambling, including online gambling, which is not purely recreational; 41 42 q. Violation of company policies and regulations for: 43 44 1. Pilferage or theft of ship’s store or cargo; 45 2. Pilferage or theft of ship’s property, of crew or passengers or other persons 46 with business at the ship; 47 3. Embezzlement of ship’s funds; 48 4. Unauthorized disposal of ship’s properties for personal gain; 49 5. Any act of dishonesty with intention to defraud the company; 50 6. Gross negligence and failure to observe proper storage and cargo handling 51 procedures resulting in delay of ships and/or damage to cargo; 52 7. Failure to observe and comply with the regulation on non-manifested 53 baggage, parcels, and cargo on board; 54 8. Failure to observe regulations on expiration of shore liberty; 55 9. Being left behind by the ship in any port without justifiable reason; 56 10. Disorderly conduct and/or disrespect towards passengers or other persons; 57 11. Immorality so as to cast aspersion on the good name of the ship and the 58 company; or 59 12. Inflicting harm or injury to others. 60 61 r. Incompetence and inefficiency of a seafarer; 62 50 s. Concerted action to breach approved contracts; 1 2 t. Any activity which tends to destroy the harmonious relationship between and 3 among the ship’s crew and/or the company; 4 5 u. Grave abuse of authority which disturbs the harmonious relationship of the ship’s 6 crew; 7 8 v. Any other case of abuse of authority; 9 10 w. For gross misbehavior prejudicial to good order and discipline; 11 12 x. Gross negligence resulting in damage, loss, spoilage or deterioration of the ship’s 13 stocks and property; 14 15 y. Any other case as to cast aspersion on the good name of the company and the 16 ship; 17 18 z. Violation to observe safety and environmental rules/ regulations; 19 20 aa. Failure to comply with the drug and alcohol policy of the company; 21 22 bb. Embezzlement of monies and/or properties of a fellow worker entrusted for delivery 23 to kin or relatives in the Philippines; 24 25 cc. Failure to refund the cost of his/her repatriation advanced by the principal/employer 26 or licensed manning agency where termination of employment was due to his/her 27 own fault; 28 29 dd. Failure or refusal by the master mariner to present employment records, such as 30 payroll and daily time records, pay slips, or other documents, such as medical 31 records, that belong or pertain to the seafarer and that is in the possession of the 32 master mariner, when required by the individual seafarer, the Secretary of the 33 DMW, the Secretary of the DOLE, their duly authorized representatives, or by the 34 duly authorized representative of a recognized organization provided that the 35 maximum fine that may be imposed for this offense shall not exceed Php 36 1,000,000.00, pursuant to R.A. No. 12021 and its IRR; or 37 38 ee. Unjustified refusal to be repatriated in case of mandatory repatriation in 39 accordance with the declaration of the Philippine government. 40 41 42 Complaints 43 44

text: 59 submit the following documents/requirements for pre-qualification: 60 61 10 A. Business Name Certificate issued by the Department of Trade and Industry (DTI), in 1 the case of a single proprietorship; or a certified copy of Securities and Exchange 2 Commission (SEC) Registration, together with a certified copy of the Articles of 3 Partnership or Articles of Incorporation duly registered with the SEC in the case of a 4 partnership or corporation, respectively; 5 6 B. Proof of financial capacity: 7 8 i. Bank certificate showing a deposit of at least Five Million Pesos (Php 9 5,000,000.00), with authority to examine the bank account; 10 11 ii. Duly filed Income Tax Returns (ITR) for the last two (2) years of the proprietor, 12 partners, members of the Board of Directors and major stockholders of a 13 corporation and official receipts showing payment of income tax; and 14 15 iii. Proof of sources of investment. 16 17 C. Proof of existence of new principal: 18 19 i. MWO verified and/or consulate authenticated MA concluded by the manning 20 agency and the new principal. 21 22 ii. Principal’s profile to include: 23 24 a. Valid business license or commercial registration of the principal with 25 English translation; 26 b. Information on business activities; 27 c. Number of years in operation; and 28 d. Number of seafarers; 29 30 D. Proof of possession by the sole proprietor, managing partner, president or chief 31 executive officer, as the case may be, of a bachelor's degree, or at least four (4) years 32 experience in shipping-related or human resource management or experience in 33 heading or managing a shipping or manpower business. 34 35 E. Valid clearances from the NBI and the Department for the proprietor, partners, and all 36 members of the board of directors of the applicant agency. 37 38 If a member of the board is a foreign national, the following shall be required: a duly 39 authenticated police clearance from his/her country of origin, and an affidavit declaring 40 no criminal conviction or no pending criminal case for illegal recruitment or a crime 41 involving moral turpitude. 42 43 F. In case of a corporation or partnership, a duly notarized undertaking by the corporate 44 officers and directors, or partners, that they shall be jointly and severally liable with 45 the corporation or partnership for claims and/or damages that may be awarded to the 46 seafarers; 47 48 G. List of all officials and personnel involved in recruitment and placement, together with 49 their appointment, signed bio-data and two (2) copies of their passport-size pictures, 50 as well as their clearances from the NBI and their individual affidavits declaring that 51 they have no conviction or pending criminal case for illegal recruitment or case 52 involving moral turpitude; 53 54 H. Certificate of attendance of the sole proprietor, managing partner, president, chief 55 executive officer and/or operations manager in the Pre-Licensing Orientation Seminar 56 (PLOS); 57 58 I. Flowchart detailing the step-by-step recruitment procedures, documentary 59 requirements, briefings and orientations required, authorized fees and costs, 60 deployment timeframes, and the responsible officer/s and process cycle time for every 61 phase of the process; and 62 11 J. Four-year business plan detailing financial, market and operational viability, including 1 projected income and a risk management plan. 2 3 The Department, in accordance with law and after consultation with the stakeholders, 4 may modify the list or prescribe additional pre-qualification requirements. 5 6

text: 22 Ships to Another LMA. The accreditation of a principal/employer and enrollment of ship 23 may be transferred to another LMA; provided, that such transfer shall be a continuation 24 of the seafarer’s contract and shall not involve diminution of wages and benefits of the 25 seafarer hired through the previous agency; and provided, further, that the transferee 26 agency shall execute an Affidavit of Assumption of Responsibility, as prescribed by the 27 Department, over all obligations of the principal/employer due to the seafarers originally 28 recruited, processed, or deployed by the former LMA, as well as other obligations arising 29 from the transfer of the ship and transfer of accreditation of principal/employer. 30 31 A succeeding LMA which seeks the accreditation of the same principal/employer from the 32 date of cancellation of the accreditation of the principal/employer with the previous LMA 33 shall be deemed a transferee agency. 34 35 For this purpose, any accreditation within six (6) months immediately following its 36 cancellation by the principal/employer shall be deemed a transfer of accreditation, in 37 which case, an assumption of responsibility of the principal/employer shall be required. 38 39

text: 21 of Vessel Enrollment. Upon the written order of the Secretary, or the latter’s duly 22 authorized representative, the accreditation and/or vessel enrollment of a 23 principal/employer shall be suspended or deactivated for a period not exceeding sixty 24 (60) calendar days, based on any of the following grounds: 25 26 a. Refusal or unreasonable failure to act on any request for assistance or 27 repatriation of distressed seafarers; 28 29 b. Deliberate violation/non-compliance of the principal/employer with its contractual 30 and/or legal obligations to its hired seafarer/s, including, but not limited to, 31 abandonment cases, harassment and bullying cases, sexual assault and sexual 32 harassment cases; 33 34 c. When found to have hired and employed a seafarer who is a minor; 35 36 d. Continued processing and deployment of the seafarers for the principal/employer 37 will lead to the further exploitation of any or all of its applicants and seafarers or 38 pose imminent danger to the lives and safety of its seafarer/s; or 39 40 e. Other circumstances analogous to the foregoing as determined by the DMW. 41 42

text: 20 applicant shall submit, prior to the issuance of the provisional license, the following post 21 qualification requirements as may be prescribed by the Department: 22 23 a. A lease contract for an office space measuring at least one hundred (100) square 24 meters, or proof of building ownership that will be used as an office or an identified 25 portion thereof measuring at least 100 square meters; 26 27 b. An office layout providing and clearly delineating adequate spaces that will serve as 28 a receiving area, interview room, administrative and finance area, 29 conference/training room, and an executive office; 30 31 c. An inventory of office equipment and facilities, which at the minimum, shall include 32 the following: 33 34 i. Office furniture such as tables and chairs for the president, other officers and 35 employees, as well as similar furniture for the receiving area, interview room 36 and conference/training room; 37 38 ii. Secured filing cabinets; and 39 40 iii. Office equipment, which at the minimum, shall include two (2) computers, 41 computer printer and scanner, photocopying machine, landline telephone, 42 internet connection, and multi-media equipment (i.e. LCD projector, and other 43 similar audio-visual equipment). 44 45 d. Organizational chart indicating the duties and responsibilities and names of officers 46 and staff, which at the minimum, shall consist of the President, Chief Executive 47 Officer, Recruitment and/or Documentation Officer, Accountant or Bookkeeper, 48 Cashier, and Liaison Officer; 49 50 e. An escrow agreement with a bank authorized by the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas 51 (BSP) to handle trust accounts, with deposit in the amount not less than One Million 52 Five Hundred Thousand Pesos (PhP 1,500,000.00); 53 54 f. Updated bank certificate stating that the escrow deposit remains at One Million Five 55 Hundred Thousand Pesos (PhP 1,500,000.00); 56 57 g. A duly notarized undertaking by the sole proprietor, the managing partner, or the 58 president of the corporation, stating that the applicant shall: 59 60 i. Select and deploy only medically fit and technically qualified recruits; 61 62 12 ii. Assume full and complete responsibility for all claims and liabilities which may 1 arise in connection with the use of the license; 2 3 iii. Assume joint and several liability with the employer/shipowner/principal for all 4 claims and liabilities which may arise in connection with the implementation of 5 the contract, including but not limited to unpaid wages, death and disability 6 compensation and repatriation; 7 8 iv. Guarantee compliance with existing labor and social legislation of the 9 Philippines and applicable regulations of the Flag State and international 10 organizations such as the International Maritime Organization (IMO) and 11 International Labour Organization (ILO); 12 13 v. Assume full and complete responsibility for all valid, legal and authorized acts 14 of its officers, employees and representatives done in connection with 15 recruitment and placement; 16 17 vi. Negotiate for the best terms and conditions of employment for its seafarers; 18 19 vii. Inform the seafarers of their rights and duties and disclose the full terms and 20 conditions of employment under their contracts of employment and articles of 21 agreement prior to and in the process of engagement; 22 23 viii. Provide orientation to the seafarers on recruitment procedures, terms and 24 conditions and other relevant information to its seafarers, and provide the 25 necessary facilities for the purpose; 26 27 ix. Act on complaints or problems brought to its attention or submit reports on the 28 status or condition of seafarers; 29 30 x. Ensure that the ship and the crew are adequately insured by the Protection and 31 Indemnity Club or similar insurance, inclusive of the compulsory insurance 32 required under R.A. No. 8042, as amended, through the submission of a 33 Certificate of Cover; 34 35 xi. Ensure that the contracts of employment are in accordance with the standard 36 employment contract and other applicable laws, regulations and collective 37 bargaining agreements; 38 39 xii. Guarantee that there is no officer or employee of the licensed manning agency 40 related within the fourth civil degree of consanguinity or affinity to any official or 41 employee of any government agency engaged, directly or indirectly, in the 42 implementation of R.A. No. 8042, as amended; 43 44 xiii. Adhere to ethical standards as prescribed in the Code of Conduct for Ethical 45 Recruitment, as endorsed by the manning industry associations and the 46 Department; 47 48 xiv. Repatriate deployed seafarers and their personal belongings when the need 49 arises or reimburse expenses of repatriation advanced by OWWA; 50 51 xv. Deploy at least seventy-five (75) seafarers to a new principal and its ship/s 52 within three (3) years from the issuance of its license; and 53 54 h. Proof of appointment of a Data Privacy Officer in compliance with R.A. No. 10173 55 and submission of non-disclosure agreements signed by each 56 staff/personnel/employees/officer of licensed manning agency pursuant to Section 57 39 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 11641. 58 59 The Department, in accordance with law and after consultation with the stakeholders, 60 may modify the list or prescribe additional post-qualification requirements. 61 62 13

text: 32 Commission by an LMA of any of the offenses below shall be a ground for the imposition 33 of the corresponding penalty. 34 35 I. SERIOUS OFFENSES. Serious offenses are punishable by cancellation of license 36 including applicable accessory penalties: 37 38 1. Deploying a seafarer who is below eighteen (18) years old or below the minimum 39 age requirement for overseas employment; 40 41 2. Engaging in acts of gross material misrepresentation for the purpose of securing 42 a license or renewal thereof, or transfer of accreditation of principal/employer 43 and/or enrollment of a ship, such as giving false information or documents; 44 3. Engaging in the recruitment or placement of seafarers for jobs harmful to public 45 health or morality or to the dignity of the Republic of the Philippines; 46 47 4. Engaging in acts of misrepresentation for the purpose of processing seafarers 48 through a crew order that pertains to: (1) non-existent work or vessel; (2) work or 49 position different from the actual overseas work; (3) position different from actual 50 work onboard; or (4) work with a different employer whether accredited or not with 51 the Department; 52 53 5. Transfer or change of ownership, directly or indirectly, of a single proprietorship 54 licensed to engage in overseas employment; 55 56 6. Charging or accepting directly or indirectly any amount of money, goods or 57 services, or any fee or bond for any purpose from an applicant seafarer; 58 59 7. Allowing a non-Filipino citizen to head or manage an LMA; 60 61 44 8. Passing on to the seafarer the fees and costs that are solely chargeable to the 1 principal/employer; 2 3 9. Becoming an officer or member of the Board of any corporation or partnership 4 engaged directly or indirectly in the management of a travel agency by any of the 5 Proponents; 6 7 10. Deploying seafarers whose employment and travel documents were not 8 processed by the Department; 9 10 11. Allowing a non-licensee or non-holder of authority to conduct recruitment and 11 placement activities on behalf of the agency; 12 13 12. Allowing a foreign national or entity to own more than 25% of the authorized capital 14 and/or participate in the management and/or operations of an LMA, except in the 15 role of a board director; 16 17 13. Unjustifiably refusing or unreasonably failing to act on any received request for 18 assistance, including request for repatriation, leading to death, severe 19 psychological impairment, sexual abuse, or maltreatment of the seafarer; 20 21 14. Committing acts of, as well as acts promoting Trafficking in Persons as defined by 22 R.A. No. 9208, as amended, and other relevant laws; 23 24 15. Conviction of a crime constituting graft and corrupt practices as defined by the 25 Revised Penal Code, R.A. Nos. 3019 and 6713, and other relevant laws, involving 26 any official, employee, or personnel of the Department; or 27 28 16. Failure to act on abandonment cases of seafarers, as defined under Part I, Rule 29 II, Item 1, herein. 30 31 II. LESS SERIOUS OFFENSES. Less serious offenses are punishable by suspension of 32 license from Six (6) Months and One (1) Day to One (1) Year plus accessory penalties, 33 or in lieu thereof, alternative penalties plus accessory penalties as may be imposed: 34 35 1. Obstructing or attempting to obstruct inspection by the Department or its duly 36 authorized representative/s; 37 38 2. Substituting or altering to the prejudice of the seafarer, employment contracts 39 approved by the Department, from the time of actual signing thereof by the parties 40 up to and including the period of expiration of the same, without the approval of the 41 Department; 42 43 3. Withholding or denying travel or other pertinent documents from an applicant 44 seafarer before departure for monetary or financial considerations, or for any other 45 reasons, other than those authorized under P.D. No. 442, as amended, and its 46 IRR; 47 48 4. Failure or refusal to present employment records, such as payroll and daily time 49 records, pay slips, or other documents, such as medical records, that belong or 50 pertain to the seafarer and that is in the possession of the LMA, when required by 51 the individual seafarer, the Secretary of the DMW, the Secretary of the DOLE, their 52 duly authorized representatives, or by the duly authorized representative of a 53 recognized organization provided that the maximum fine that may be imposed for 54 this offense shall not exceed Php 1,000,000.00, pursuant to R.A. No. 12021 and 55 its IRR; 56 57 5. Failure to furnish a seafarer with copies of all his/her pertinent medical reports 58 and/or medical records at no cost to the seafarer; 59 60 6. Engaging in recruitment activities in places other than that specified in the license 61 or branch authority without an SRA; 62 45 7. Appointing or designating agents, representatives, or employees without notice to 1 and acknowledgement from the Department within the period prescribed under the 2 Rules; 3 4 8. Withholding of seafarer’s salaries or remittances or allotments, SSS contributions, 5 and loan amortization or shortchanging/reduction thereof without justifiable 6 reasons; 7 8 9. Allowing persons who are otherwise disqualified from participating in the overseas 9 employment program under existing laws, rules and regulations to participate in 10 the management and operation of the agency; 11 12 10. Failure to reimburse necessary expenses incurred and paid by the seafarer in 13 connection with his documentation and processing for purposes of deployment, in 14 cases where an employment contract has been signed and deployment does not 15 take place without the seafarer's fault; 16 17 11. Failure to comply with any of the undertakings submitted to the Department; 18 19 12. Imposing a compulsory and exclusive arrangement whereby a seafarer is required 20 to undergo health examinations (PEME), training, seminar, instruction or schooling 21 of any kind only from specifically designated institutions, entities, or clinics, as the 22 case may be, unless the cost is shouldered by the principal/employer or LMA; 23 24 13. Imposing a compulsory and exclusive arrangement whereby a seafarer is required 25 to avail of a loan from a specifically designated institution, entity, or person; 26 27 14. Granting a loan to a seafarer with interest exceeding eight percent (8%) per annum 28 which will be used for payment of legal and allowable fees and making the seafarer 29 issue, either personally or through a guarantor or accommodation party, post-dated 30 checks in relation to the said loan; 31 32 15. Refusal to condone or renegotiate a loan incurred by the seafarer after the latter’s 33 employment contract has been prematurely terminated through no fault of the 34 seafarer; 35 36 16. Failure to submit reports on serious/critical incidents involving abandonment, 37 piracy, death, missing seafarer, terrorism, hijacking, armed robbery, harassments, 38 serious illness, and injury requiring repatriation; 39 40 17. Failure to comply with the required notice and reportorial requirement to the 41 seafarer's family or next of kin in case of critical and/or medical incidents under 42 R.A. No. 12021 and its IRR; 43 44 18. Falsifying or altering travel documents of an applicant seafarer in relation to 45 recruitment and deployment; 46 47 19. Failure to actually deploy a contracted seafarer within the validity of the OFW 48 Clearance without valid reason; 49 50 20. Engaging in any acts of misrepresentation in connection with recruitment and 51 placement of seafarers, such as furnishing or publishing any false notice, 52 information or document in relation to recruitment or employment; or 53 54 21. Violation of any or all of the provisions of R.A. No. 12021 and its IRR. 55 56 Habitual Commission of Less Serious Offenses. – An LMA which commits the same less 57 serious offense for five (5) times or more shall be considered a habitual offender. For this 58 purpose, the maximum of the penalty shall be imposed upon it. 59 60 46 III. LIGHT OFFENSES. Light offenses are punishable by Two (2) Months to Six (6) 1 Months suspension of license plus accessory penalties, or in lieu thereof, alternative 2 penalties plus accessory penalties as may be imposed: 3 4 1. Failure to submit other reports as required under R.A. No. 12021 and its IRR, these 5 Rules and other issuances of the Department, as well as other applicable laws, 6 rules and regulations; 7 8 2. Influencing any person or entity not to employ any seafarer who has not applied 9 for employment through the agency, or influencing any person or entity not to 10 employ any seafarer who has formed, joined, supported, contacted or is supported 11 by any union or workers’ organization; 12 13 3. Failure to report to the Department the termination or resignation of 14 representatives or employees within the prescribed period; 15 16 4. Failure to monitor and submit quarterly report on the status and condition of its 17 deployed seafarers; or 18 19 5. Violations of other pertinent provisions of these Rules and other relevant laws, 20 rules and regulations, guidelines and other issuances on recruitment and 21 placement of seafarers for overseas employment and the protection of their 22 welfare. 23 24

text: 47 Catering in Ocean-Going Ships. 48 49 Accommodation and Recreational Facilities. Unless otherwise exempted herein or by 50 appropriate regulations, all ocean-going ships shall provide and maintain safe, decent, 51 and adequate accommodations, including but not limited to recreational facilities, for 52 overseas seafarers that meet the minimum standards set out in MLC 2006, as amended, 53 taking into account the need to protect the health and well-being of seafarers working or 54 living on board the ship, or both. There shall be a basic shipboard facility for women, such 55 as separate sleeping rooms. 56 57 Requirements for Sanitation. All ocean-going ships shall have sanitary facilities that 58 meet the minimum standards for health and hygiene, which shall be accessible to 59 overseas seafarers on board, and sanitation standards set by Chapter XV - "Port, Airport, 60 Vessel and Aircraft Sanitation" of P.D. No. 856, otherwise known as the "Code on 61 29 Sanitation of the Philippines", and its IRR. The sanitary facilities shall be located in 1 convenient places, allowing easy access to work stations. 2 3 Separate sanitary facilities shall also be provided for male and female crew members of 4 the ship. 5 6 Food and Catering. All ocean-going ships shall ensure the protection and promotion of 7 the health of the overseas seafarers. Shipowners shall ensure that their ocean-going 8 ships carry on board and serve food and drinking water of appropriate quality, nutritional 9 value, and quantity that adequately cover the requirements of the ship and take into 10 consideration the differing cultural and religious backgrounds of the seafarers on board 11 the ship. Seafarers on board a ship shall be provided with food free of charge during the 12 period of engagement. The food, drinks, and the preparation thereof shall be in 13 accordance with the standards of P.D. No. 856, pertinent laws, rules, regulations, and 14 international standards. In all cases, the ship's cooks shall be trained and qualified for 15 their position. 16 17

text: 27 Partner. Unless otherwise provided in the Articles of Partnership, the license of a 28 partnership shall be automatically revoked upon the death or withdrawal of a partner 29 which materially interrupts the course of business or results in the dissolution of the 30 partnership. The surviving partner/s shall, within ten (10) calendar days, report such death 31 or withdrawal to the Department. Failure to report shall automatically include the surviving 32 partner/s in the list of persons with a derogatory record. 33 34

text: 58 Shipping Enterprises (POSE) which own/manage/operate Philippine flag ship trading 59 overseas may apply for a special license subject to the submission of the following 60 requirements: 61 21 a. Proof of payment of a non-refundable filing fee of Fifty Thousand Pesos 7 (PhP50,000.00). 8 9 The Department, in accordance with law and after consultation with the stakeholders, 10 may modify the list or prescribe additional requirements for the application for a special 11 license. 12 13

text: 54 consultation involving the seafarers and the private sector, shall determine, formulate and 55 establish minimum, separate and distinct SECs for seafarers, in accordance with 56 accepted international standards and maritime practices. These SECs, which shall be 57 reviewed periodically to keep them attuned to international requirements and demands, 58 shall be the minimum requirement in every individual contract approved by the 59 Department. 60 61 40 The Department may also develop ship category, skills, or country-specific SECs to better 1 address peculiarities and needs of seafarers. Likewise, a special contract for cadets may 2 be developed. 3 4 The SEC between the shipowner and the overseas seafarer shall be in writing and shall 5 include, but not be limited to, the following information and terms subject to R.A. No. 6 10173: 7 8 (a) Seafarer’s full name, date of birth, age, birthplace, permanent or residential 9 address; 10 11 (b) SRB Number, Seafarer’s Identity Document Number, Seafarer’s Registration 12 Number, and other necessary documentation as may be required by appropriate 13 government agencies; 14 15 (c) Shipowner’s name and address; 16 17 (d) LMA’s name and address; 18 19 (e) Place and date of execution of the SEC; 20 21 (f) Duration of the contract; 22 23 (g) Point of hire; 24 25 (h) Capacity in which the seafarer is to be employed; 26 27 (i) Amount of the seafarer’s salary, and the formula used for calculating the same; 28 29 (j) Maximum hours of work and minimum hours of rest; 30 31 (k) Wages and wage-related benefits, which include, but are not limited to, overtime 32 pay, holiday pay, vacation leave pay, premium pay, paid leaves, and 13th month 33 pay, if applicable; 34 35 (l) Compensation and benefits for occupational injury, sickness, or death; 36 37 (m)The mandatory process for the determination of the final and binding disability 38 grading to be determined by a DOH-accredited third doctor who is an expert in the 39 disability of the seafarer, in case of conflict between the findings of the company 40 and the seafarer’s doctor; 41 42 (n) Social security and welfare benefits, including compulsory insurance coverage as 43 provided under Section 37-A of R.A. No. 8042, as amended by R.A. No. 10022, 44 and Section 6(n) of R.A. No. 11641; 45 46 (o) Stipulations on repatriation or similar undertakings; 47 48 (p) Separation pay and retirement pay, if applicable; 49 50 (q) Reference to the CBA, or other similar instruments, if applicable; 51 52 (r) Other benefits provided by law, company policy, or CBA; and 53 54 (s) Termination of the contract and the conditions thereof, including: 55 56 (1) If the contract is for an indefinite duration, the conditions entitling either party to 57 terminate it and the period to give notice which shall not be less for the 58 shipowner than for the seafarer; 59 60 (2) If the contract is for a definite duration, the date fixed for its expiry; and 61 62 41 (3) If the contract is for a voyage, the port of destination and the time which has to 1 expire after arrival before the seafarer’s discharge. 2 3 The SEC shall be in English, or translated in the language known to the seafarer, and 4 executed in four (4) original copies before the commencement of the employment. 5 6 The shipowner and the seafarer shall each have a signed original of the agreement. In 7 addition, a signed original shall be made available on board the ship. 8 9 Likewise, the SEC shall be part of the discussion in the PDOS. 10 11

text: 55 an allotment which shall be payable once a month to the seafarer's designated allottee in 56 the Philippines through any authorized Philippine bank. 57 58 The employer shall provide the seafarer with facilities for the seafarer’s designated 59 allottee/s at no expense to the seafarer. The allotment shall be at least eighty percent 60 42 (80%) of the seafarer's monthly salary. The monthly salary shall consist of basic wage 1 plus fixed or guaranteed overtime. 2 3 The employer may also provide facilities for the seafarer to remit any amount earned in 4 excess of the seafarer's allotment, including backwages, if any, to the seafarer's 5 designated allottee/s in the Philippines through any authorized Philippine bank. 6 7 The allotments shall be paid to the designated allottee/s in Philippine currency at the rate 8 of exchange at the time of remittance indicated in the credit advice of the local authorized 9 Philippine bank, which information shall be provided to the designated allottee/s. 10 11 Where a seafarer is held captive on or off the ship as a result of acts of piracy, armed 12 robbery, acts of aggression, war-related violence, terrorism, or other acts of violence 13 against ships or its crew, or any form of detention of the ship, the wages and other 14 entitlements under the seafarer’s employment agreement, SEC or relevant CBA, 15 including the remittance of any allotments, shall continue to be paid during the entire 16 period of captivity and until the seafarer is released and duly repatriated at the point of 17 hire, or where the seafarer dies while in captivity, until the date of death as determined 18 under Philippine law. 19 20

text: 21 Abandonment, Sickness, Injury, Disappearance, Death or Other Causes. In 22 accordance with the MLC 2006, as amended, on financial security for vessels, the 23 shipowner shall provide an expeditious and effective financial security system to assist 24 seafarers in the event of their abandonment and to assure compensation for contractual 25 claims in the event of the seafarer’s sickness, injury, disappearance, death or other 26 causes occurring while they are serving under a seafarer’s employment contract, or 27 arising from their employment under such agreement. 28 29 The certificate or other documentary evidence of financial security cover shall contain the 30 following information: 31 32 (a) Name of the ship; 33 (b) Port of registry of the ship; 34 (c) Call sign of the ship; 35 (d) IMO number of the ship 36 (e) Name and address of the provider or providers of the financial security; 37 (f) Contact details of the persons or entity responsible for handling seafarers’ request 38 for relief; 39 (g) Name of the shipowner; 40 (h) Period of validity of the financial security; and 41 (i) An attestation from the financial security provider that the financial security meets 42 the requirements of the MLC 2006, as amended. 43 44

text: 26 Notwithstanding the provisions on compulsory insurance coverage as required by law, 27 the repatriation of a seafarer or the latter’s remains, the payment of salaries, benefits, 28 and/or other forms of assistance during detention subject to appropriate guidelines as 29 determined by the DMW, and the transport of personal effects shall be the primary 30 responsibility of the principal/employer and the deploying LMA. This entails the obligation 31 to cover repatriation and attendant costs, including airfare and immigration 32 fines/penalties. This obligation shall be without prior determination of the cause of the 33 need to repatriate the seafarer. After the seafarer has returned to the country, the 34 principal/employer or LMA may, however, recover the cost of repatriation from the 35 seafarer if the termination of the employment was due solely to the seafarer’s fault. 36 37

text: 35 (OPC) due to the Death or Temporary or Permanent Incapacity of the 36 Incorporator/Sole Stockholder. Within thirty (30) calendar days after the death or 37 incapacity of the incorporator/sole stockholder, the documentary requirements prescribed 38 by the Department should be submitted. 39 40 Failure to report the death or incapacity of the incorporator within the 30-day period given 41 shall cause the revocation of the OPC’s license and the inclusion of the nominee in the 42 derogatory record. 43 44 The nominee or the alternative nominee of the OPC shall manage and operate the LMA 45 in case of the death or incapacity of the incorporator/sole stockholder of the OPC; 46 Provided, that the nominee and/or the alternative nominee must have all the 47 qualifications and none of the disqualifications to engage in the business of recruitment; 48 Provided that, the designated legal heirs of the single stockholder have been lawfully 49 determined, and the heirs have designated one of them or have agreed that the estate 50 shall be the single stockholder of the OPC , as provided by the applicable law; Provided 51 further, that the designated single stockholder of the OPC has all the qualifications and 52 none of the disqualifications under these Rules. 53 54 Once a temporary incapacity ends, the incorporator/sole stockholder can resume the 55 management and operation of the OPC upon duly informing the SEC and 56 acknowledgment of the Department. 57 58

Within thirty (30) calendar days after the death or 37 incapacity of the incorporator/sole stockholder, the documentary requirements prescribed 38 by the Department should be submitted. 39 40 Failure to report the death or incapacity of the incorporator within the 30-day period given 41 shall cause the revocation of the OPC’s license and the inclusion of the nominee in the 42 derogatory record. 43 44 The nominee or the alternative nominee of the OPC shall manage and operate the LMA 45 in case of the death or incapacity of the incorporator/sole stockholder of the OPC; 46 Provided, that the nominee and/or the alternative nominee must have all the 47 qualifications and none of the disqualifications to engage in the business of recruitment; 48 Provided that, the designated legal heirs of the single stockholder have been lawfully 49 determined, and the heirs have designated one of them or have agreed that the estate 50 shall be the single stockholder of the OPC , as provided by the applicable law; Provided 51 further, that the designated single stockholder of the OPC has all the qualifications and 52 none of the disqualifications under these Rules. 53 54 Once a temporary incapacity ends, the incorporator/sole stockholder can resume the 55 management and operation of the OPC upon duly informing the SEC and 56 acknowledgment of the Department. 57 58

text: 54 55 a. Fees Chargeable to Principal/Employer: 56 57 1. Manning Fees - LMAs shall charge from their principal/employer a manning fee 58 to cover services rendered in the recruitment and deployment of seafarers. 59 60 22 2. Processing Fees - All processing fees required for deployment such as Pre- 1 Employment Medical Examination (PEME), regardless of the results thereof, 2 and medical reports in the principal’s/employer’s designated clinic, DMW and 3 OWWA fees, visas, principal’s/employer’s flag State ship requirements, 4 principal’s/employer’s required trainings and other requirements. 5 6 However, in case of seafarer’s failure or unjustified refusal to join ship after all 7 processing fees have been incurred by the principal/employer, said fees shall 8 be refunded, except recruitment or placement fees, visas and other related 9 costs, by the seafarer within thirty (30) calendar days from demand. 10 11 b. Costs Chargeable to the Seafarer. Documentation costs of all statutory 12 requirements such as, but not limited to, passport, Seafarer’s Record Book (SRB), 13 Seafarer’s Identification Card (SID), training mandated by applicable Philippine 14 laws and regulations, NBI/police/barangay clearance, and birth certificate. 15 16

In case an additional escrow is required from the licensed agency 8 due to its pending recruitment violation case/s, it shall maintain the escrow deposit of One 9 Million Five Hundred Thousand Pesos (Php1,500,000.00) plus the additional escrow. 10 11 Those with existing licenses shall, within one (1) year from effectivity hereof, increase 12 their escrow deposit to One Million Five Hundred Thousand Pesos (Php1,500,000.00). 13 14 The escrow deposit shall answer for all valid and legal claims arising from contracts of 15 employment and violations of the conditions for the grant and use of the license, including 16 fines imposed by the Department. The escrow shall likewise guarantee compliance with 17 prescribed recruitment procedures, rules and regulations, appropriate terms and 18 conditions of employment, and relevant issuances of the Department. 19 20 The escrow deposit is separate and distinct from the minimum capitalization requirement. 21 22 In case the escrow deposit has been diminished or depleted, the licensed manning 23 agency shall replenish the escrow deposit within thirty (30) calendar days from receipt of 24 the Notice to Replenish Escrow Deposit. Failure to comply with such Notice will result in 25 the suspension of the license of the manning agency until such time the agency has 26 complied with the Notice to Replenish. 27 28

text: Applications for Transfer of Accreditation of a 51 Principal/Employer and/or Enrollment of a Ship with Objections due to Outstanding 52 Obligations. The pendency of the claims for money or enforcement of obligations arising 53 out of business relations between principal/employer and their existing LMA shall not 54 prevent the Department from acting on the request for transfer of accreditation of a 55 principal/employer and/or enrollment of a ship, if public interest so requires. 56 57 Aside from non-compliance with Section 121 (Transfer of Accreditation of 58 Principal/Employer and Enrollment of Ships to Another LMA), the transfer of accreditation 59 may not be processed under any of the following circumstances: 60 61 35 a. There are several pending requests for the processing of seafarers filed before 1 the Department; 2 3 b. There are applicant seafarers who already signed their respective employment 4 contracts, issued with employment visas and completed all of their needed 5 documents for deployment; 6 7 c. There are pending requests for the repatriation of its seafarers; 8 9 d. The transferee agency has failed to secure the required NOL from the transferor 10 agency; or 11 12 e. In any analogous case where the applicants and its seafarers will be prejudiced 13 by the immediate transfer of accreditation. 14 15 The Department shall return the submitted documentary requirements to the transferee 16 agency without prejudice to refiling once the transferee agency has secured the required 17 NOL from the transferor agency, subject to the submission of updated documentary 18 requirements. 19 20 The transfer of accreditation may be given due course upon resolution of the above issues 21 and/or circumstances. 22 23 As a matter of policy, the Department will not intervene on the dispute of the parties arising 24 from the objection. Hence, the parties may discuss and settle the reason/s for the 25 objection or elevate their dispute to the court of law which has jurisdiction over the matter. 26 In such cases, the original agency shall retain full and complete responsibility over the 27 contractual obligations of the original principal. 28 29 Should the said court of law decide or determine that the accusations leveled by an 30 agency to another is frivolous in nature, the Department may motu proprio initiate the 31 necessary disciplinary action against the erring agency. 32 33

text: 42 Penalties. Commission by a foreign principal/employer of any of the offenses below shall 43 be a ground for the imposition of the corresponding penalty. 44 45 I. SERIOUS OFFENSES. Serious offenses are punishable by permanent disqualification 46 and delisting from the roster of accredited principals/employers: 47 48 a. Gross violation of laws, rules, and regulations on overseas employment; 49 50 b. Gross negligence leading to serious injury or illness or death of the seafarer; 51 52 c. Grave misconduct against the seafarer; 53 54 d. Non-payment or underpayment of wages and benefits; 55 56 e. Commission of any act by the principal/employer or its agent or representative 57 against the seafarer, which is tantamount or amounts to a felony, crime, or offense 58 punishable by the laws of the Philippines or by the port state/flag state; 59 60 f. Compelling a seafarer to work for another employer or perform another work; 61 47 g. Passing on or deducting from a seafarer’s salary the payment of the cost of 1 insurance fees, premium, or other insurance related charges as provided under 2 the compulsory insurance coverage; 3 4 h. Deliberate concealment or failure to inform the LMA of significant events adversely 5 affecting the welfare, well-being, status, or condition of its hired seafarers; 6 7 i. Abandonment of Seafarers, as defined under Part I, Rule II, Item 1 herein; or 8 9 j. Unlawful transfer of seafarers to another vessel without approval from the 10 Department. 11 12 II. LESS SERIOUS OFFENSES. Less serious offenses are punishable by Six (6) Months 13 to one (1) year suspension from participation in the overseas employment program, or in 14 lieu thereof, the alternative penalty of fine may be imposed as prescribed under the DMW 15 Adjudication Rules and Procedures and R.A. No. 12021 and its IRR: 16 17 a. Default on its other contractual obligations to the seafarer and/or to its manning 18 agent; 19 20 b. Substitution of the DMW-approved contract; 21 22 c. Withholding of the seafarer’s travel and other pertinent documents; 23 24 d. Violation of other laws, rules and regulations on recruitment and placement; 25 26 e. Failure or refusal to present employment records, such as payroll and daily time 27 records, pay slips, or other documents, such as medical records, that belong or 28 pertain to the seafarer and that is in the possession of the foreign 29 principal/employer, when required by the individual seafarer, the Secretary of the 30 DMW, the Secretary of the DOLE, their duly authorized representatives, or by the 31 duly authorized representative of a recognized organization provided that the 32 maximum fine that may be imposed for this offense shall not exceed Php 33 1,000,000.00, pursuant to R.A. No. 12021 and its IRR; 34 35 f. Failure to monitor and submit quarterly reports on the welfare, well-being, status 36 or condition relating to its hired seafarer to the Department; 37 38 g. Failure to monitor and submit reports within five (5) calendar days from being 39 informed of the incident on significant or critical incidents to the Department; 40 41 h. Failure to provide periodic bi-monthly reports of the action taken to the seafarer’s 42 family or next of kin and the DMW as applicable until the matter is resolved, in 43 relation to the significant or critical incidents; or 44 45 i. Any other violation of the rights of the seafarers provided under R.A. No. 12021 46 and its IRR. 47 48 49 Disciplinary Action Against Seafarer, Classification of Offenses and Penalties 50 51

text: 59 themselves in the most professional, responsible, and ethical manner in the performance 60 of their duties and fulfill the following obligations: 61 59 A. DUTY TO FAMILY: 1 2 1) To provide financial and moral support to the seafarer’s family in the Philippines; 3 and 4 5 2) To communicate with the seafarer’s family as often as possible. 6 7 B. DUTY TO FELLOW SEAFARER: 8 9 1) To assist and cooperate with other crew on board the same ship; and 10 11 2) To conduct themselves professionally and refrain from engaging in and/or 12 encouraging any acts of harassment and bullying against their fellow crew or their 13 superior on board ships or onshore. 14 15 C. DUTY TO COUNTRY: 16 17 1) To uphold the ideals of the Republic of the Philippines and to defend it, if warranted; 18 19 2) To abide by the rules and regulations aimed at promoting the seafarer’s interest 20 and enhancing national gains; and 21 22 3) To be ambassadors of goodwill for the Philippines, demonstrating the best traits of 23 the Filipinos and refraining from any acts that may tarnish the Filipino image 24 abroad. 25 26 D. DUTY TO MANNING AGENCY AND/OR PRINCIPAL/EMPLOYER: 27 28 1) To provide the correct and true statements/certifications regarding the seafarer’s 29 skill, experience, and other qualifications; 30 31 2) To abide by the terms and conditions of the employment contract; 32 33 3) To maintain a high level of productivity as well as abide by company rules and 34 regulations; and 35 36 4) To refrain from committing acts which are detrimental to the interest of the 37 seafarer’s principal, employer, or LMA once a contract has been executed by the 38 seafarer and/or during the seafarer’s employment. 39 40 E. DUTY TO PORT STATE AND FLAG STATE: 41 42 To respect and obey the laws, morals, customs, and traditions of the port state and the 43 flag state. 44 45 46 INCENTIVES AND AWARDS 47 48

The DMW shall provide legal assistance in accordance with the guidelines 42 provided for the use of the Agarang Kalinga at Saklolo para sa mga OFW na 43 Nangangailangan (AKSYON) Fund. 44 45 For seafarers with cases before the Department, the Department shall advise the seafarer 46 that he or she may secure the services of counsel and if he or she so chooses, refer him 47 or her to the Public Attorney’s Office (PAO) who shall render, free of charge, legal 48 representation, assistance, and counseling to seafarers. In the event that the seafarer 49 does not avail of the services of counsel, the Overseas Employment Adjudicator shall 50 endeavor to protect the seafarer’s rights. 51 52 For seafarers who have cases abroad, the shipowner and/or concerned LMA shall have 53 the primary obligation to inform the DMW of the nature of the case to enable it to provide 54 legal assistance in accordance with the guidelines provided for the use of the AKSYON 55 Fund. 56 57

text:
. Execution of decisions, orders or awards. 
a. The Secretary of Labor and Employment or any Regional Director, the Commission or any Labor 
Arbiter, or Med-Arbiter or Voluntary Arbitrator may, motu proprio or on motion of any interested 
party, issue a writ of execution on a judgment within five (5) years from the date it becomes final 
and executory, requiring a sheriff or a duly deputized officer to execute or enforce final decisions, 
orders or awards of the Secretary of Labor and Employment or regional director, the Commission, 
the Labor Arbiter or med-arbiter, or voluntary arbitrators. In any case, it shall be the duty of the 
responsible officer to separately furnish immediately the counsels of record and the parties with 
copies of said decisions, orders or awards. Failure to comply with the duty prescribed herein shall 
subject such responsible officer to appropriate administrative sanctions. 
   
b. The Secretary of Labor and Employment, and the Chairman of the Commission may designate 
special sheriffs and take any measure under existing laws to ensure compliance with their 
decisions, orders or awards and those of the Labor Arbiters and voluntary arbitrators, including the 
imposition of administrative fines which shall not be less than P500.00 nor more than P10,000.00. 
(As amended by Section 13, Republic Act No. 6715, March 21, 1989)

text:
. Establishment and formulation of skills standards. There shall be national skills standards for 
industry trades to be established by the Council in consultation with employers’ and workers’ organizations 
and appropriate government authorities. The Council shall thereafter administer the national skills standards.

text:
. Improved offer balloting. In an effort to settle a strike, the Department of Labor and Employment 
shall conduct a referendum by secret ballot on the improved offer of the employer on or before the 30th day of 
the strike. When at least a majority of the union members vote to accept the improved offer the striking 
workers shall immediately return to work and the employer shall thereupon readmit them upon the signing of 
the agreement. 
58 
Downloaded from the Department of Labor and Employment, Republic of the Philippines - 29 September 2011  
http://www.dole.gov.ph/labor_codes.php?id=34 
 
 
In case of a lockout, the Department of Labor and Employment shall also conduct a referendum by secret 
balloting on the reduced offer of the union on or before the 30th day of the lockout. When at least a majority of 
the board of directors or trustees or the partners holding the controlling interest in the case of a partnership 
vote to accept the reduced offer, the workers shall immediately return to work and the employer shall 
thereupon readmit them upon the signing of the agreement. (Incorporated by Section 28, Republic Act No. 
6715, March 21, 1989)

text:
. Prohibition on certification election. The Bureau shall not entertain any petition for certification 
election or any other action which may disturb the administration of duly registered existing collective 
bargaining agreements affecting the parties except under Articles 253, 253-A and 256 of this Code. (As 
amended by Section 15, Republic Act No. 6715, March 21, 1989)

text:
. Qualifications of health personnel. The physicians, dentists and nurses employed by employers 
pursuant to this Chapter shall have the necessary training in industrial medicine and occupational safety and 
health. The Secretary of Labor and Employment, in consultation with industrial, medical, and occupational 
safety and health associations, shall establish the qualifications, criteria and conditions of employment of such 
health personnel.

text:
. Unfair labor practices of labor organizations. It shall be unfair labor practice for a labor 
organization, its officers, agents or representatives: 
a. To restrain or coerce employees in the exercise of their right to self-organization. However, a labor 
organization shall have the right to prescribe its own rules with respect to the acquisition or 
retention of membership; 
   
b. To cause or attempt to cause an employer to discriminate against an employee, including 
discrimination against an employee with respect to whom membership in such organization has 
been denied or to terminate an employee on any ground other than the usual terms and conditions 
under which membership or continuation of membership is made available to other members; 
   
c. To violate the duty, or refuse to bargain collectively with the employer, provided it is the 
representative of the employees; 
   
d. To cause or attempt to cause an employer to pay or deliver or agree to pay or deliver any money or 
other things of value, in the nature of an exaction, for services which are not performed or not to be 
performed, including the demand for fee for union negotiations; 
   
e. To ask for or accept negotiation or attorney’s fees from employers as part of the settlement of any 
issue in collective bargaining or any other dispute; or 
   
f. To violate a collective bargaining agreement.  
The provisions of the preceding paragraph notwithstanding, only the officers, members of governing boards, 
representatives or agents or members of labor associations or organizations who have actually participated in, 
authorized or ratified unfair labor practices shall be held criminally liable. (As amended by Batas Pambansa 
Bilang 130, August 21, 1981) 
53

text:
. Private sector participation in the recruitment and placement of workers. Pursuant to national 
development objectives and in order to harness and maximize the use of private sector resources and 
initiative in the development and implementation of a comprehensive employment program, the private 
employment sector shall participate in the recruitment and placement of workers, locally and overseas, under 
such guidelines, rules and regulations as may be issued by the Secretary of Labor.

text:
. Prohibited acts. 
a. It shall be unlawful for any employer:  
   
1. To deny any woman employee the benefits provided for in this Chapter or to discharge any 
woman employed by him for the purpose of preventing her from enjoying any of the 
benefits provided under this Code. 
   
2. To discharge such woman on account of her pregnancy, or while on leave or in 
confinement due to her pregnancy; 
   
3. To discharge or refuse the admission of such woman upon returning to her work for fear 
that she may again be pregnant.

text:
. Sponsoring of apprenticeship program. Any of the apprenticeship schemes recognized herein may 
be undertaken or sponsored by a single employer or firm or by a group or association thereof or by a civic 
organization. Actual training of apprentices may be undertaken: 
a. In the premises of the sponsoring employer in the case of individual apprenticeship programs;  
b. In the premises of one or several designated firms in the case of programs sponsored by a group or 
association of employers or by a civic organization; or  
c. In a Department of Labor and Employment training center or other public training institution.

text:
. Employees’ Compensation Commission. 
a. To initiate, rationalize, and coordinate the policies of the employees’ compensation program, the 
Employees’ Compensation Commission is hereby created to be composed of five ex-officio 
members, namely: the Secretary of Labor and Employment as Chairman, the GSIS General 
Manager, the SSS Administrator, the Chairman of the Philippine Medical Care Commission, and 
the Executive Director of the ECC Secretariat, and two appointive members, one of whom shall 
represent the employees and the other, the employers, to be appointed by the President of the 
Philippines for a term of six years. The appointive member shall have at least five years 
experience in workmen’s compensation or social security programs. All vacancies shall be filled for 
the unexpired term only. (As amended by Section 19 [c], Executive Order No. 126) 
   
b. The Vice Chairman of the Commission shall be alternated each year between the GSIS General 
Manager and the SSS Administrator. The presence of four members shall constitute a quorum. 
Each member shall receive a per diem of two hundred pesos for every meeting that is actually 
attended by him, exclusive of actual, ordinary and necessary travel and representation expenses. 
In his absence, any member may designate an official of the institution he serves on full-time basis 
as his representative to act in his behalf. (As amended by Section 2, Presidential Decree No. 
32 
Downloaded from the Department of Labor and Employment, Republic of the Philippines - 29 September 2011  
http://www.dole.gov.ph/labor_codes.php?id=34 
 
 
1368) 
   
c. The general conduct of the operations and management functions of the GSIS or SSS under this 
Title shall be vested in its respective chief executive officers, who shall be immediately responsible 
for carrying out the policies of the Commission. 
   
d. The Commission shall have the status and category of a government corporation, and it is hereby 
deemed attached to the Department of Labor and Employment for policy coordination and 
guidance. (As amended by Section 2, Presidential Decree No. 1368)

. Employees’ Compensation Commission. 
a. To initiate, rationalize, and coordinate the policies of the employees’ compensation program, the 
Employees’ Compensation Commission is hereby created to be composed of five ex-officio 
members, namely: the Secretary of Labor and Employment as Chairman, the GSIS General 
Manager, the SSS Administrator, the Chairman of the Philippine Medical Care Commission, and 
the Executive Director of the ECC Secretariat, and two appointive members, one of whom shall 
represent the employees and the other, the employers, to be appointed by the President of the 
Philippines for a term of six years. The appointive member shall have at least five years 
experience in workmen’s compensation or social security programs. All vacancies shall be filled for 
the unexpired term only. (As amended by Section 19 [c], Executive Order No. 126) 
   
b. The Vice Chairman of the Commission shall be alternated each year between the GSIS General 
Manager and the SSS Administrator. The presence of four members shall constitute a quorum. 
Each member shall receive a per diem of two hundred pesos for every meeting that is actually 
attended by him, exclusive of actual, ordinary and necessary travel and representation expenses. 
In his absence, any member may designate an official of the institution he serves on full-time basis 
as his representative to act in his behalf. (As amended by Section 2, Presidential Decree No.

c. The general conduct of the operations and management functions of the GSIS or SSS under this 
Title shall be vested in its respective chief executive officers, who shall be immediately responsible 
for carrying out the policies of the Commission.

deemed attached to the Department of Labor and Employment for policy coordination and

text:
. Definitions. 
a. "Worker" means any member of the labor force, whether employed or unemployed. 
   
b. "Recruitment and placement" refers to any act of canvassing, enlisting, contracting, transporting, 
utilizing, hiring or procuring workers, and includes referrals, contract services, promising or 
advertising for employment, locally or abroad, whether for profit or not: Provided, That any person 
or entity which, in any manner, offers or promises for a fee, employment to two or more persons 
shall be deemed engaged in recruitment and placement. 
   
c. "Private fee-charging employment agency" means any person or entity engaged in recruitment and 
placement of workers for a fee which is charged, directly or indirectly, from the workers or 
employers or both. 
   
d. "License" means a document issued by the Department of Labor authorizing a person or entity to 
operate a private employment agency. 
   
e. "Private recruitment entity" means any person or association engaged in the recruitment and 
placement of workers, locally or overseas, without charging, directly or indirectly, any fee from the 
workers or employers. 
   
3 
Downloaded from the Department of Labor and Employment, Republic of the Philippines - 29 September 2011  
http://www.dole.gov.ph/labor_codes.php?id=34 
 
 
f. "Authority" means a document issued by the Department of Labor authorizing a person or 
association to engage in recruitment and placement activities as a private recruitment entity. 
   
g. "Seaman" means any person employed in a vessel engaged in maritime navigation. 
   
h. "Overseas employment" means employment of a worker outside the Philippines. 
   
i. "Emigrant" means any person, worker or otherwise, who emigrates to a foreign country by virtue of 
an immigrant visa or resident permit or its equivalent in the country of destination.

text:
. Offenses. Offenses penalized under this Code and the rules and regulations issued pursuant 
thereto shall prescribe in three (3) years. 
All unfair labor practice arising from Book V shall be filed with the appropriate agency within one (1) year from 
accrual of such unfair labor practice; otherwise, they shall be forever barred.

text:
. First-aid treatment. Every employer shall keep in his establishment such first-aid medicines and 
equipment as the nature and conditions of work may require, in accordance with such regulations as the 
Department of Labor and Employment shall prescribe. 
The employer shall take steps for the training of a sufficient number of employees in first-aid treatment.

text:
. Definitions. As used in this Title: 
a. "Manpower" shall mean that portion of the nation’s population which has actual or potential capability 
to contribute directly to the production of goods and services. 
   
b. "Entrepreneurship" shall mean training for self-employment or assisting individual or small industries 
within the purview of this Title.

text:
. Prohibition against aliens; exceptions. All aliens, natural or juridical, as well as foreign 
organizations are strictly prohibited from engaging directly or indirectly in all forms of trade union activities 
without prejudice to normal contacts between Philippine labor unions and recognized international labor 
centers: Provided, however, That aliens working in the country with valid permits issued by the Department of 
Labor and Employment, may exercise the right to self-organization and join or assist labor organizations of 
their own choosing for purposes of collective bargaining: Provided, further, That said aliens are nationals of a 
country which grants the same or similar rights to Filipino workers. (As amended by Section 29, Republic Act 
No. 6715, March 21, 1989)

text:
. Ineligibility of managerial employees to join any labor organization; right of supervisory 
employees. Managerial employees are not eligible to join, assist or form any labor organization. Supervisory 
employees shall not be eligible for membership in a labor organization of the rank-and-file employees but may 
join, assist or form separate labor organizations of their own. (As amended by Section 18, Republic Act No. 
6715, March 21, 1989)

text:
. Right to weekly rest day. 
a. It shall be the duty of every employer, whether operating for profit or not, to provide each of his 
employees a rest period of not less than twenty-four (24) consecutive hours after every six (6) 
consecutive normal work days. 
   
b. The employer shall determine and schedule the weekly rest day of his employees subject to 
collective bargaining agreement and to such rules and regulations as the Secretary of Labor and 
Employment may provide. However, the employer shall respect the preference of employees as to 
their weekly rest day when such preference is based on religious grounds.

text:
. Non-transferability of license or authority. No license or authority shall be used directly or indirectly 
by any person other than the one in whose favor it was issued or at any place other than that stated in the 
license or authority be transferred, conveyed or assigned to any other person or entity. Any transfer of 
business address, appointment or designation of any agent or representative including the establishment of 
additional offices anywhere shall be subject to the prior approval of the Department of Labor.

text:
. Exemption from levy, tax, etc. All laws to the contrary notwithstanding, the State Insurance Fund 
and all its assets shall be exempt from any tax, fee, charge, levy, or customs or import duty and no law 
hereafter enacted shall apply to the State Insurance Fund unless it is provided therein that the same is 
applicable by expressly stating its name. 
Chapter IX 
RECORDS, REPORTS AND PENAL PROVISIONS

text:
. Transfer of lands to tenant-workers. Being a vital part of the labor force, tenant-farmers on private 
agricultural lands primarily devoted to rice and corn under a system of share crop or lease tenancy whether 
classified as landed estate or not shall be deemed owner of a portion constituting a family-size farm of five (5) 
hectares, if not irrigated and three (3) hectares, if irrigated. 
In all cases, the land owner may retain an area of not more than seven (7) hectares if such landowner is 
cultivating such area or will now cultivate it.

text:
-A. Terms of a collective bargaining agreement. Any Collective Bargaining Agreement that the 
parties may enter into shall, insofar as the representation aspect is concerned, be for a term of five (5) years. 
No petition questioning the majority status of the incumbent bargaining agent shall be entertained and no 
certification election shall be conducted by the Department of Labor and Employment outside of the sixty-day 
period immediately before the date of expiry of such five-year term of the Collective Bargaining Agreement. All 
other provisions of the Collective Bargaining Agreement shall be renegotiated not later than three (3) years 
after its execution. Any agreement on such other provisions of the Collective Bargaining Agreement entered 
into within six (6) months from the date of expiry of the term of such other provisions as fixed in such 
Collective Bargaining Agreement, shall retroact to the day immediately following such date. If any such 
agreement is entered into beyond six months, the parties shall agree on the duration of retroactivity thereof. In 
case of a deadlock in the renegotiation of the Collective Bargaining Agreement, the parties may exercise their 
rights under this Code. (As amended by Section 21, Republic Act No. 6715, March 21, 1989)

text:
. Aptitude testing of applicants. Consonant with the minimum qualifications of apprentice-applicants 
required under this Chapter, employers or entities with duly recognized apprenticeship programs shall have 
primary responsibility for providing appropriate aptitude tests in the selection of apprentices. If they do not 
have adequate facilities for the purpose, the Department of Labor and Employment shall perform the service 
free of charge.

text:
. Death. 
a. Under such regulations as the Commission may approve, the System shall pay to the primary 
beneficiaries upon the death of the covered employee under this Title, an amount equivalent to his 
monthly income benefit, plus ten percent thereof for each dependent child, but not exceeding five, 
beginning with the youngest and without substitution, except as provided for in paragraph (j) of 
Article 167 hereof: Provided, however, That the monthly income benefit shall be guaranteed for 
five years: Provided, further, That if he has no primary beneficiary, the System shall pay to his 
secondary beneficiaries the monthly income benefit but not to exceed sixty months: Provided, 
finally, That the minimum death benefit shall not be less than fifteen thousand pesos. (As amended 
by Section 4, Presidential Decree No. 1921) 
   
b. Under such regulations as the Commission may approve, the System shall pay to the primary 
beneficiaries upon the death of a covered employee who is under permanent total disability under 
this Title, eighty percent of the monthly income benefit and his dependents to the dependents’ 
pension: Provided, That the marriage must have been validly subsisting at the time of disability: 
Provided, further, That if he has no primary beneficiary, the System shall pay to his secondary 
beneficiaries the monthly pension excluding the dependents’ pension, of the remaining balance of 
the five-year guaranteed period: Provided, finally, That the minimum death benefit shall not be less 
than fifteen thousand pesos. (As amended by Section 4, Presidential Decree No. 1921) 
   
c. The monthly income benefit provided herein shall be the new amount of the monthly income benefit 
for the surviving beneficiaries upon the approval of this decree. (As amended by Section 8, 
Presidential Decree No. 1368) 
   
d. Funeral benefit. - A funeral benefit of Three thousand pesos (P3,000.00) shall be paid upon the 
death of a covered employee or permanently totally disabled pensioner. (As amended by Section 
3, Executive Order No. 179)  
Chapter VIII 
PROVISIONS COMMON TO INCOME BENEFITS

text:
. Record of death or disability. 
a. All employers shall keep a logbook to record chronologically the sickness, injury or death of their 
employees, setting forth therein their names, dates and places of the contingency, nature of the 
contingency and absences. Entries in the logbook shall be made within five days from notice or 
knowledge of the occurrence of the contingency. Within five days after entry in the logbook, the 
employer shall report to the System only those contingencies he deems to be work-connected. 
   
b. All entries in the employer’s logbook shall be made by the employer or any of his authorized official 
after verification of the contingencies or the employees’ absences for a period of a day or more. 
Upon request by the System, the employer shall furnish the necessary certificate regarding 
information about any contingency appearing in the logbook, citing the entry number, page number 
and date. Such logbook shall be made available for inspection to the duly authorized 
representative of the System. 
   
c. Should any employer fail to record in the logbook an actual sickness, injury or death of any of his 
employees within the period prescribed herein, give false information or withhold material 
information already in his possession, he shall be held liable for fifty percent of the lump sum 
equivalent of the income benefit to which the employee may be found to be entitled, the payment 
of which shall accrue to the State Insurance Fund. 
   
d. In case of payment of benefits for any claim which is later determined to be fraudulent and the 
employer is found to be a party to the fraud, such employer shall reimburse the System the full 
amount of the compensation paid.  
38
